outcome_contract.why: German and Italian visitors get a mixed-language experience — translated product pages but English navigation, consent banner, footer links, and trust strip. This undermines trust, especially on legal pages where language matters.
outcome_contract.intended_outcome_statement: All persistent UI chrome (header, footer, consent banner, trust strip) displays in the visitor's selected language

engineering_coverage.applicable: Add DE/IT to code constant; JSON chrome removed → defaults active
engineering_coverage.current_or_option_state: Add DE/IT to JSON; extend materializer
engineering_coverage.gap_or_implication: Add DE/IT to defaults; add merge to getChromeContent
engineering_coverage.carry_forward_or_notes: No component changes; translations live in `contentPacket.ts`

engineering_coverage.applicable: Full DE/IT locale page experience delivered
engineering_coverage.carry_forward_or_notes: All 4 chrome surfaces correct for DE/IT after deploy

engineering_coverage.applicable: N/A — static strings, no audit needed

engineering_coverage.current_or_option_state: Same test, but also need materializer regen test
engineering_coverage.carry_forward_or_notes: One focused test file: `contentPacket.test.ts` covering all 5 groups × 3 locales

engineering_coverage.applicable: Remove `chrome` from JSON (or let materializer overwrite); defaults become canonical
engineering_coverage.gap_or_implication: Keep JSON chrome as override surface; defaults as base

engineering_coverage.applicable: No change — cached read, additive object keys
Additive constant change + JSON key removal; revert = delete `de`/`it` keys from constant

stage_payload.decision_summary: All four Caryina chrome components (Header, SiteFooter, ConsentBanner, ShippingReturnsTrustBlock) are already locale-wired — they all call `getChromeContent(lang)`. The display bug is caused entirely by missing DE/IT translation values in the chrome data layer. The decision here is: **where should DE/IT chrome translations live and how should they be loaded?**
goals: Choose an approach that delivers DE/IT translations for all 27 chrome strings across 5 groups
Ensure the fix is durable across future materializer re-runs
Add test coverage confirming locale-correct output from `getChromeContent`
non_goals: Translating product copy, policy body text, or SEO content
Adding new chrome components or changing chrome structure
constraints: Materializer (`materialize-site-content-payload.ts`) must continue to work without changes (or with minimal additive changes only)
Any fix must survive future materializer re-runs without silently wiping translations
Translations must be natural (not machine-literal) German and Italian
assumptions: Chrome strings are UI micro-copy that belongs in code constants, not in a CMS or content packet

evaluation_criteria.Why it matters: The materializer could silently wipe DE/IT if chrome remains in JSON
How easy it is to update or extend translations in future
Can changes be reverted safely in production

options_considered.Description: Add DE/IT to `CHROME_EN_DEFAULTS` in `contentPacket.ts`; remove `chrome` from `site-content.generated.json` so defaults are always used
options_considered.Upside: Zero materializer risk; chrome translations are code-owned, versioned with code; simple rollback; no merge logic
options_considered.Downside: All future chrome changes require code deploy (not data update)

options_considered.Description: Add DE/IT entries to JSON chrome; extend materializer's `SiteContentPayload` type and `buildPayload()` to preserve/regenerate chrome with translations
options_considered.Upside: Chrome stays close to product content data; materializer-driven regeneration
options_considered.Downside: Materializer must now carry translations (requires source of truth for DE/IT strings); significant materializer changes; risk of mistranslation on regeneration
options_considered.Key risks: Materializer becomes translation-aware — adds scope and fragility

options_considered.Description: Add DE/IT to `CHROME_EN_DEFAULTS`; change `getChromeContent` to deep-merge JSON chrome over defaults (so JSON can override per-string)
options_considered.Upside: Most flexible; both code and JSON can contribute locale values
options_considered.Downside: Adds merge function complexity; two sources of truth for chrome; harder to reason about locale resolution
options_considered.Viable?: Viable but unnecessary complexity for current scope

rejected_approaches: **Option B (JSON + materializer guard)** — Materializer responsibility mismatch; requires chrome translation source in content packet; over-scoped for UI micro-copy
**Option C (per-string merge)** — Unnecessary complexity; no use case for per-string JSON overrides on UI chrome labels

validation_implications: `getChromeContent("de")` must return German strings for all 27 fields
`getChromeContent("it")` must return Italian strings for all 27 fields
`getChromeContent("en")` must continue returning English strings unchanged
Existing component tests that mock `getChromeContent` must still pass (no interface change)
sequencing_constraints: TASK-01 (author translations) must precede TASK-02 (add to constant)

TASK-02 and TASK-03 must be in the same commit — atomically swap source of truth from JSON to code; do not leave chrome in JSON while code also has DE/IT (this would cause the JSON chrome to still win, hiding the new translations until TASK-03 is done)
TASK-04 (tests) can be written after TASK-02/03

process_topology.note: None: no material process topology change The runtime locale-read path is unchanged: `[lang]/layout.tsx` calls `getChromeContent(lang)`, components receive strings, locale resolution happens inside `contentPacket.ts`. After this fix, `CHROME_DEFAULTS` replaces `CHROME_EN_DEFAULTS` as the active chrome source (because the JSON `chrome` key is removed), and `localizedText(value, locale)` returns the correct locale string instead of falling back to `en`. **Important source-of-truth change**: Chrome translations permanently move from JSON to code (`contentPacket.ts`). Future contributors updating
